item.content:encoded:
All the weird, potentially tone-breaking things I'm seeing complaints about just add to the experience for me. Tell the kid I'd watch his show, even if it runs a risk of erasing me or itself from reality.

Congratulations on +100. I'd say "don't let it go to your head" but at this point I'm sure you're used to all the well-deserved praise your articles get.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But how will The Foundation know if the people they sent are actual Foundation members or just a fictional character? Or which part of this scip is fictional at all?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I tend to have a preference for the somewhat quirky SCPs. One thing in particular I don't understand, though, is why they're just leaving their diplomats stranded on the station. Yes, the engines are a part of the anomaly, but so far there's no indication that they haven't worked, and it would seem to me more sensible to use them a last time to evacuate personnel from an unstable location in spacetime, especially if they're looking to contain the reality bender (who might decide to do something quite punitive if he gets upset). The Foundation can't treat its personnel as casually expendable, or they won't have any personnel left.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Snuck a little mention of the Pattern Screamer in there, I see.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't like it. I knew what the twist was half way through the article, and the random reference to 2747 didn't contribute anything other than revealing that twist to me. I knew it was gonna involve meta stuff the moment that was included. I'm suffering from meta fatigue, to be honest, and this doesn't really help matters much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm a sucker for Star Trek — I basically grew up on TNG and Voyager. As a result, I really enjoyed this as a send-up of that genre. Adding in the idea of

+ show block
– hide block

preteen / teenage fanfic plus reality bender


was pretty enjoyable, too, as was the grimdark stuff.

This SCP is kind of silly, but its reasons for being silly make sense internally. +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I read it as

+ show block
– hide block

The reality bender doesn't know about the foundation, he made up the whole thing in the first place.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Rather than finding it intriging, the hook breaks my suspension of disbelief as-is. It'd be more believeable IMO if…

Spoiler
Can't dance around it this time without it being super-awkward

The Foundation came to the SCP under their own power. Once there, the author gets the "inspiration" of the Foundation and starts incorporating it into their story.

Simply put, I find the idea that a reality bender simply "knows" about the Foundation without any real context or hints as to why to be unbelievable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What you're not understanding is that…

+ Show whatever
- Hide whatever

The Foundation isn't consciously sending exploration teams to this thing. They never actually created the "Scranton Reality Drives" or "Scranton Reality Cannons" for use with this thing.

The reality warping pre-teen (accidentally) created those as part of his narrative about the Space Station. One day, the Foundation woke up, and this entire fleet of Scranton Reality Drive ships and cannons were just suddenly there, complete with crews and a backstory and everything. And the Foundation slowly started to realize they weren't there all along, they weren't supposed to be there, they had been reality-warped in.

The hook is: how does the reality warping YA writer know about the Foundation and Scranton technology?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm a sucker for space skips, but I do have a couple questions regarding it.

In the article, it mentions "The Foundation as itself". So does this mean that Desmond created the Foundation through latent reality bending? I remember there being another skip like that in Series 3, the number escapes me now though.

Others have mentioned the Scranton Anchors, how would this person know about them? That would be a massive breach of infosec.

I really like it, but those details are pretty critical in my opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But fair enough; I entirely understand your dislike of this one

I just think that some of the specifics can be altered to allow the Foundation to "naturally" explore this thing than apparently get entrapped by it from the get-go. Putting the station closer to Earth (within the Solar System, but not around Earth) should solve the immediate problems- it's close enough to possibly threaten humanity within a relatively short time as well as allowing the Foundation to mount an exploration team.

Heck, although you'll need to rework it a bit, you could incorporate the above into the main narrative- the station (which may have been seen as unremarkable aside from the fact that it's a goddamn space station) suddenly blips from the Solar System to it's orbit around Cygnus. That would certainly be worth sending an exploration team when it becomes clear that the station had undergone some major changes while in transit; probes can only do so much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I admit to having a bit of meta fatigue, but this skip took me pleasantly by surprise in how it developed. Nicely done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would alter the containment procedures so it doesn't say "several." Perhaps "no less than (x)." Sounds a bit more professional. Excellent job, though!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well:

Spoilers
Spoilers

The implication which (hopefully) comes forward was that Sykes has been altering the Foundation as well as the station, incorporating them into his narrative. Hence the revelation at the end that he created the deliberately ridiculous Cannon/Engine (designed here to serve as a parody of the ridiculousness of Scranton overuse). This is the real reason for the Foundation's behaviour; they're being sucked into this kid's story, and so their behaviour is mildly altered to compensate.

But fair enough; I entirely understand your dislike of this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Several long-range Scranton Reality Cannons

Ya lost me here and well, failed to really regain my interest afterwards. I get what's going on, but there just seems to be a fundamental disconnect there.

The station is about 6,100 LY from Earth, more-or-less doing it's own thing. I see no reason for the Foundation's involvement needing to be any more than "Fling a FTL observation probe to it, tell NASA to edit the thing out if they spot it, and just observe it in case it tries anything funny before Earth reaches nonanomalous spaceflight." At the very least, diplomatic contact with alien species presents one hell of a coverup problem should humanity make "first contact" at a later date. Yeah, they may not be real in this case, but still…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

I -almost- downvoted based on the Scranton Realty overuse, but the ending did indeed change my mind.

+ Show whatever
- Hide whatever

I couldn't get behind a galactic space station, but I could get behind a latent reality warper (who doesn't realize he's a reality warper) creating a galactic space station without realizing it, as he writes fiction. It's unnerving because he knows about the Scranton stuff, and he's been unknowingly affecting the Foundation as well.

All of these individuals have developed significant personal problems, including large quantities of adultery and substance abuse.

That's an odd sentence. I'm not sure "adultery" would be considered a quantitative thing. Maybe, "many instances of adultery."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's a starman waiting in the sky
He'd like to come and meet us
But he thinks he'd blow our minds
There's a starman waiting in the sky
He's told us not to blow it
Cause he knows it's all worthwhile

I really should have done more to this (Hippo pointed out a number of problems and flaws with it which I should have addressed more closely), but at a certain point you just get burnt out on a skip and need to post. I ended up feeling no desire to do any more to this. I imagine this won't do amazingly, but I'll at least see if there are people who enjoy it.

Anyway; many thanks to all those who looked over this (I've lost my notes on who exactly that was, so my apologies :p.).The picture is in the public domain, and can be found here.

Oh, and anyone who's thinking of downvoting on principle because of my shameless abuse of Scranton Reality stuff/the ridiculous physics, there's a very specific reason for such lunacy, so read to the end. It's not just laziness.

This skip also contains references to 2669 and 2747. If the authors of either of these pieces would like me to remove the references, I will happily do so.

Oh, and all credit to NineVolt to finding the picture.

You can find more of my stuff here.
